title: The man whose diapers turned black — and 40 years later, his joints
blurb: Urine that darkens on standing, a lifetime later a bluish ear and a crumbling spine. One missing enzyme, two very different clues decades apart.

patient.complaint: Progressive low-back and knee pain; his wife noticed dark staining in the toilet and bluish discoloration of his ears.

opening: A 48-year-old man presents with years of worsening back and large-joint pain. On exam you notice slate-blue pigment in the cartilage of both ears and a bluish tinge to the sclerae. His mother recalls his infant diapers 'turned black if left overnight.' A fresh urine sample looks normal but darkens to brown-black after sitting out. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Otherwise healthy; no medications. Family: a brother had 'early arthritis.' Consanguineous grandparents. No liver disease, no neurologic symptoms, normal cognition and development.
exam: Bluish-gray pigmentation of ear and nasal cartilage (ochronosis) and sclerae. Restricted, painful lumbar spine; large-joint crepitus. No hepatomegaly. Neuro exam normal.

prompts.cause: Which enzyme is deficient, and what accumulates?
prompts.mechanism: Why the black urine AND the joint disease decades apart?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the enzyme step that is deficient in this disease.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Correct — homogentisate oxidase (HGD). Without it, homogentisic acid backs up, spills into urine (where it oxidizes/polymerizes to a black pigment on standing), and slowly deposits as ochronotic pigment in cartilage and discs → the pigmentation and the destructive arthropathy. It's autosomal recessive and, notably, benign in childhood apart from the dark diapers.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite — the block is in tyrosine DEGRADATION, one step past homogentisate. It's the enzyme whose loss lets homogentisic acid accumulate.
pinpoint.revealMsg: The answer was HGD (Homogentisate → Fumarylacetoacetate): homogentisate oxidase.

mcqs.stem: Why does the urine only turn black AFTER standing (or when alkalinized), not immediately?
choices: Homogentisic acid must be oxidized and polymerized (favored by air and alkaline pH) to the dark pigment
Bacteria in the toilet metabolize it
mcqs.expl: Fresh homogentisic acid is colorless; on exposure to air (and faster at alkaline pH) it oxidizes and polymerizes to a melanin-like black pigment — the classic 'darkens on standing' clue. Historically it also reduces Benedict's reagent, mimicking a reducing sugar.

mcqs.stem: Contrast alkaptonuria with tyrosinemia type I, the block one step further down (FAH).
choices: Both cause fatal neonatal liver failure
Alkaptonuria (HGD) is essentially benign apart from late arthritis/pigment; tyrosinemia I (FAH) makes toxic fumarylacetoacetate/succinylacetone → infant liver failure
Alkaptonuria causes liver failure; tyrosinemia I is benign
mcqs.expl: The intermediate that accumulates determines severity: homogentisic acid is relatively innocuous (slow pigment deposition), whereas the downstream fumarylacetoacetate and succinylacetone in FAH deficiency are acutely hepatotoxic.

mcqs.stem: A proposed treatment for alkaptonuria is nitisinone. How would blocking 4-HPPD (upstream) help?
choices: It replaces the missing HGD enzyme
It reduces production of homogentisate by inhibiting the enzyme just upstream of it, lowering pigment load (at the cost of raising tyrosine)
mcqs.expl: Nitisinone inhibits 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase, which sits just upstream of homogentisate — so less homogentisic acid is made and less ochronotic pigment deposits. The trade-off is elevated tyrosine, requiring dietary restriction.
